coredata.dc:title: Green and fast microwave-driven synthesis of fluorescent N,S-carbon dots for determination of two 5-nitroimidazole antimicrobials
coredata.dc:creator: Osman, Mohamed M.
El-Shaheny, Rania
Ibrahim, Fawzia A.
coredata.dc:description: A green, fast, and economical technique for the manufacture of N,S-CQDS has been devised based on 3-min microwave irradiation of citric acid and thiosemicarbazide that furnished highly fluorescent quantum dots (λex/em of 360/430 nm) with high water solubility and stability. Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Zeta potential analysis, Fourier transform infrared, UV-Visible spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to fully characterize the N,S-CQDS. The obtained N,S-CQDS are spherical shaped with average size of 13.2 ± 4.3 nm and Zeta potential of -32.46 ± 1.55 mv. The effect of UV light and pH on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N,S-CQDS were also investigated. The synthesized N,S-CQDS were used as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of two 5-nitroimidazole antimicrobials; secnidazole (SNZ) and tinidazole (TNZ) by fluorescence turn-off via inner filter effect and static quenching mechanisms. This approach demonstrated a linear dependency of the fluorescence signal on the concentration of both drugs over the range of 15-200 μM. The limit of detection and limit of quantification were 2.18 and 6.62 μM, respectively, for SNZ, and 2.29 and 6.96 μM, respectively, for TNZ. The developed method was used to determine both drugs in pharmaceutical dosage forms and human urine samples with % recovery of 100.65 ± 1.35 for SNZ, 98.66 ± 0.47 for TNZ in pharmaceutical dosage forms and 102.24 ± 2.93 for SNZ, 98.2 ± 1.78 for TNZ in case of spiked human urine samples. The approach's selectivity was confirmed for determining both analytes in the presence of various co-existing and co-administered species. The method validity was assessed in accordance with ICH recommendations. Various metrics were used to evaluate the greenness of the proposed probe. The developed probe is environmentally-friendly due to the quick synthesis of luminous N,S-CQDS using affordable and low-energy microwave radiation, and the absence of organic solvents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Secnidazole
Tinidazole
Microwave-driven synthesis
N,S-carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared N,S -CQDS
Procedures for determining SNZ and TNZ
General procedures for constructing calibration curves
Determination of SNZ and TNZ in their tablets
Analysis of SNZ and TNZ in human urine
Characterization and optical features of N,S-CQDS synthesized with microwave assistance
SNZ and TNZ determination using N,S-CQDS as a fluorescent sensor
Analytical procedure optimization for SNZ and TNZ determination
Analysis of SNZ and TNZ in their commercial tablets
Determination of SNZ and TNZ in spiked human urine samples
Comparison of the N,S-CQDS fluorescence sensor approach with the methods published for the investigated drugs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Structural formula of SNZ and TNZ and (B) Schematic representation of N,S-CQDS synthesis starting with citric acid and thiosemicarbazide within 3 minutes of irradiation using 800 W microwave followed by centrifugation and successive filtration.
Effect of microwave irradiation time on the fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized N,S-CQDS.
(A) TEM image of the synthesized N,S- CQDS, (B) FT-IR spectroscopy, (C) EDX spectroscopy, and (D) Zeta potential analysis of the prepared CQDS.
XPS spectra of (A) survey scan, (B) N1s, (C) O1s, (D) C1s, and (E) S2p of N,S-CQDS.

(A) Excitation/emission spectra of N,S- CQDS and UV-Visible spectra of SNZ, TNZ, and N,S- CQDS, (B) excitation dependent emission behavior of CQDS at λex ranging from (300-400 nm), and (C) pH effect on N,S- CQDS fluorescence.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S- CQDS in presence of increasing concentrations of SNZ ranging from (0-200 μM) and the inset shows the corresponding calibration curve, and (B) fluorescence emission spectra of N,S- CQDS in presence of increasing concentrations of TNZ ranging from (0-200 μM) and the inset shows the corresponding calibration curve.

(A) % Suppressed efficiency of the observed and corrected Fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDS upon addition of various concentrations of SNZ, (B) % suppressed efficiency of the observed and corrected Fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDS upon addition of various concentrations of TNZ, (C) Stern-Volmer's curves of N,S-CQDS – SNZ system at different temperatures, (D) Stern-Volmer's curves of N,S-CQDS – TNZ system at different temperatures, and (E) UV-Visible absorption spectrum of CQDS in absence and presence of SNZ and TNZ (measured against drug as the reference).

Effects of (A) PH, (B) N,S-CQDS amount on fluorescence quenching of N,S- CQDS by SNZ and TNZ, (C) UV- Visible absorption spectra of SNZ at different pH values, and (D) UV- Visible absorption spectra of TNZ at different pH values, and (E) Equilibrium of SNZ and TNZ with their nitronate tautomer under basic condition.
Application of N,S-CQDS for determination of (A) SNZ and (B) TNZ in human urine samples.
(A) Complex GAPI and (B) Agree results for greenness evaluation of the developed method.
Comparison between heat- and microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-CQDS.
Results of Stern-Volmer analysis of N,S-CQDS – SNZ and -TNZ systems at different temperatures.
Analytical data of the proposed method.
Comparison of the results for determination of SNZ and TNZ in their pure form and commercial tablets using the proposed and comparison methods.

row.entry: Comparison methods (El Walily et al., 2000; Lopez-Martinez et al., 1997)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Numbers between parenthesis are t and F tabulated values at P = 0.05 (Miller and Miller, 2018).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intra-day and inter-day precision for SNZ and TNZ assay by the proposed method.
Selectivity results of N,S-CQDS showing the effect of different co-existing species on its fluorescence intensity.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
F
1 and F
2 are the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDS without and with co-administered drugs and excipients respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of SNZ and TNZ in human urine samples.
Comparison of the proposed method with the reported methods for determination of SNZ and TNZ.

row.entry:
C18 column, mobile phases consisting of water and methanol (30:70), at pH of 3.5.
UV detection at 310 nm
C18 column, mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile–aqueous 5 mm sodium acetate (30:70, v/v) containing of 0.1% acetic acid adjusted to pH 4.0, UV-detection at 319 nm.
Reduction of SNZ with zinc dust/HCl then diazotization and coupling with β-naphthol to give red colored chromogens measured at 510 nm.
Hydrothermal synthesis of N-CDS at 240 °C for 8 h
Synthesis of biomass carbon dots@molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescent sensor using hydrothermal technique
Charge-transfer complexation of reduced forms of TNZ and chloranilic acid.
Alkaline hydrolysis of TNZ in 0.1 N NaOH and absorbance was measured at 368 nm
C18 column, mobile phase consisting of methanol and 0.06 M sodium dihydrogen phosphate (65 : 35, v/v) adjusted to pH 4.5, UV detection at 260 nm
C18 column, mobile phase consisting of 0.025 mol/L potassium dihydrogen phosphate (containing triethylamine 0.05%, v/v) and acetonitrile (80 : 20, v/v)
silica gel modified carbon paste electrode (SG/CPE) as an electrochemical sensor
Quenching of N,S- CQDS fluorescence prepared via microwave assisted synthesis starting with CA and TSC at 360/430 nm.

head.ce:title: Green and fast microwave-driven synthesis of fluorescent N,S-carbon dots for determination of two 5-nitroimidazole antimicrobials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green, fast, and economical technique for the manufacture of N,S-CQDS has been devised based on 3-min microwave irradiation of citric acid and thiosemicarbazide that furnished highly fluorescent quantum dots (λex/em of 360/430 nm) with high water solubility and stability. Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Zeta potential analysis, Fourier transform infrared, UV-Visible spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to fully characterize the N,S-CQDS. The obtained N,S-CQDS are spherical shaped with average size of 13.2 ± 4.3 nm and Zeta potential of -32.46 ± 1.55 mv. The effect of UV light and pH on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N,S-CQDS were also investigated. The synthesized N,S-CQDS were used as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of two 5-nitroimidazole antimicrobials; secnidazole (SNZ) and tinidazole (TNZ) by fluorescence turn-off via inner filter effect and static quenching mechanisms. This approach demonstrated a linear dependency of the fluorescence signal on the concentration of both drugs over the range of 15-200 μM. The limit of detection and limit of quantification were 2.18 and 6.62 μM, respectively, for SNZ, and 2.29 and 6.96 μM, respectively, for TNZ. The developed method was used to determine both drugs in pharmaceutical dosage forms and human urine samples with % recovery of 100.65 ± 1.35 for SNZ, 98.66 ± 0.47 for TNZ in pharmaceutical dosage forms and 102.24 ± 2.93 for SNZ, 98.2 ± 1.78 for TNZ in case of spiked human urine samples. The approach's selectivity was confirmed for determining both analytes in the presence of various co-existing and co-administered species. The method validity was assessed in accordance with ICH recommendations. Various metrics were used to evaluate the greenness of the proposed probe. The developed probe is environmentally-friendly due to the quick synthesis of luminous N,S-CQDS using affordable and low-energy microwave radiation, and the absence of organic solvents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, nanotechnology has been widely applied in a variety of fields such as tissue engineering, drug delivery, semiconductors, bioimaging, pathogen recognition, biocatalysis, food industry, water treatment, agriculture, energy-related applications, aerospace industry, and robotics (Hasan et al., 2018; Paluszkiewicz et al., 2021; Krukemeyer et al., 2015). Furthermore, nanomaterials have been also used in a variety of applications, including electrochemistry and photochemistry (Saleh, 2016).

Nanomaterials can be generally grouped into two major classes, i.e., organic and inorganic nanomaterials. Inorganic-based nanomaterials include metal and metal oxide/hydroxide nanoparticles (Haghighi et al., 2021). Metal nanoparticles are mostly used as antimicrobial agents due to their long-term stability and excellent biocompatibility. On the other hand, organic-based nanomaterials are made of organic matter away from inorganic species. However, due to miscellaneous applications and numerous studies, carbon nanomaterials are considered as a distinguished category of nanomaterial that includes fullerenes, carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and nanohorns (Datta et al., 2022). Owing to the possibility of chemical functionalization of a wide variety of carbon-based nanomaterials with various functional groups, the fields of their applications are very expanded. In this vein, carbon nanomaterials exhibiting outstanding physical, chemical, and optical properties are employed to develop highly sensitive sensors for numerous analytical applications (Al-Khateeb et al., 2023; El-Shaheny et al., 2020; Shekarbeygi et al., 2021; Han et al., 2019).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDS) are a type of nanomaterial that has garnered a lot of attention as an analytical sensor due to low toxicity, eco-friendliness, simple routes of synthesis, high aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and surface passivation that aids in controlling their physicochemical properties (Lim et al., 2015). CQDS can be synthesized via two main synthesis approaches: (I) top-down approach by transforming bulk structures into nano-sized particles via dry and wet crushing processes, and (II) bottom-up approach by constructing nanomaterials through self-assembling of small molecules via different routes such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, and dry heating routes (Devi et al., 2023). The latter approach is widely used due to its simplicity, less production of waste, and production of homogenous nanoparticles. However, the techniques used in bottom-up approach suffer from certain drawbacks such as time consumption (may exceed 24 h), use of hazardous organic solvents, and need for very high temperature up to 300 °C (Chahal et al., 2021; Ng et al., 2021). Furthermore, CQDS could be dopped with heteroatoms like nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, and boron to improve their electrical, chemical, and optical properties (Ansari et al., 2021).

In this study, N,S-CQDS were synthesized within 3 minutes using a green one-pot ultrafast microwave-aided pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and thiosemicarbazide (TSC). Despite some methods for synthesizing N, S-CQDS from these starting materials have been disclosed (Belal et al., 2021; Magdy et al., 2021; Chandra et al., 2020; El Sharkasy et al., 2023), however, these reports used heat-driven hydrothermal treatment, which requires a very lengthy reaction time that takes 5-24 h and very high temperatures up to 180 °C which constrains their wide applicability. On the other hand, the proposed method is ultra-fast requiring only 3 minutes, ecofriendly with low energy consumption, depend on a widely available and frequently used home microwave, and has a minor environmental impact. These values imply a prominent novelty and high importance to the newly designed N,S-CQDS. Besides, the synthesized N, S-CQDS demonstrated great fluorescence intensity, outstanding water solubility, and high stability, enhancing their use as a fluorescent sensor in pharmaceutical application.

The N, S-CQDS generated was successfully used to quantify two commonly used antiprotozoal and antibacterial drugs secnidazole (SNZ) and tinidazole (TNZ) in pharmaceutical dosage forms and human urine samples. Secnidazole (1-(2-methyl-5-nitroimidazol-1-yl) propan-2-ol, Fig. 1 A) and tinidazole (1-(2-ethylsulfonylethyl)-2-methyl-5-nitro-imidazole, Fig. 1A) are 5-nitroimidazole derivatives used in the treatment of amebiasis, giardiasis, bacterial vaginosis, and anaerobic bacterial infections (Wisher, 2012). Different analytical methods were applied for SNZ determination such as HPLC (El Walily et al., 2000; Li et al., 2007), spectrophotometry (El Walily et al., 2000; Saffaj et al., 2006), and spectrofluorimetry (Yang et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022). Reviewing the literature showed that different analytical approaches were reported for TNZ determination including spectrofluorimetry (Yang et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022; Magdy et al., 2023), spectrophotometry (Adegoke et al., 2010; Lopez-Martinez et al., 1997), HPLC (Walash et al., 2013; Yin et al., 2013), and electrochemical methods (Sawkar et al., 2022). These methods are time-consuming, requiring expensive apparatuses, tedious pre-analysis work, and using harmful organic solvents. Thus, there is a strong need for a simple and greener technique for determination of these widely used drugs. Microwave-aided synthesis of N,S-CQDS as a fluorescent sensor is faster, simpler, greener, less expensive, and more economic in comparison with the reported methods. Furthermore, it is sensitive and selective for determination of the two 5-nitroimidazoles in their pharmaceutical products and human urine samples.

Secnidazole pure powder was kindly donated by European Egyptian Pharmaceuticals Co. (Alexandria, Egypt), Tinidazole pure powder was kindly provided by Sigma Pharmaceutical Company (Cairo, Egypt), Fladazole® film coated tablets (Amoun pharmaceutical Co. (Al Obour, Al Qalyubia, Egypt) labelled to contain 500 mg SNZ per tablet and Protozole® film coated tablets (Medical Union Pharmaceuticals, Nasr City, Cairo, Egypt), each tablet are claimed to contain 500 mg TNZ, were purchased from a local pharmacy, Delta Pharma S.A.E (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt) donated spiramycin powder, European Egyptian Pharmaceuticals Co. (Alexandria, Egypt) donated aceclofenac powder, and EIPICO Co. (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt) kindly donated paracetamol powder. El Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co provided citric acid, and acetonitrile HPLC grade
≥
99.9% was provided by Fisher Scientific, while Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA) provided thiosemicarbazide, sodium hydroxide, boric acid, sodium citrate, magnesium sulphate, calcium chloride, quinine sulphate, sucrose, carboxy methyl cellulose, glucose, sodium chloride, and potassium chloride. Adwic Co. (Cairo, Egypt) supplied acetic acid (99.85%) and phosphoric acid (86.5%). All the chemicals utilized were of analytical grade, and simply distilled water was used.
Urine sample was obtained from a healthy male volunteer (27 Years old) instructed to be withdrawn from any medicines a week before. The sample was kept at -20 till use after gentle thawing.

Fluorescence measurements were recorded with an Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA 95051, United States) Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a Xenon flash lamp and a 1 cm cuvette at a voltage of 800 V, a scan speed of 600 nm/min, and excitation/emission monochromator slit widths of 5 nm. JENWAY 6850 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Cole Parmer, UK) was used for spectrophotometric measurements. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) Model Talos L120C G2-TEM, Thermo Fisher, Waltham, Massachusetts, U.S.A was used for imaging the N,S-CQDS. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) analysis was done using Thermo Fisher Scientific (MA, USA) Nicolet-iS10 FT-IR Spectrometer with attenuated total reflectance (ATR). The zeta potential of the N,S-CQDs was determined using the Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Panalytical, Ltd, Worcestershire, UK). Elemental mapping was captured using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis for the SEM (Oxford X-Max 20). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected on K-ALPHA (Themo Fisher Scientific, USA) with monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation -10 to 1350 eV spot size 400 micrometer at pressure 10-9 millibar with full spectrum pass energy 200 eV and at narrow spectrum 50 eV. Microwave 800 Watts SAMSUNG (MG32J5133AM/GY), centrifuge (model 2-16P, Germany), Wisd Wise Clean WUC A22H Ultrasonic Bath (Witeg, Germany), and Water still DESA 0081 Vidrio Industrial POBEL (Spain) were utilized.

Standard stock solutions of SNZ and TNZ were made by dissolving accurately weighted amounts of pure SNZ and TNZ powder in separate 100 mL volumetric flasks, which were then filled with distilled water to obtain a final concentration of 2.0 mM of each drug. The N,S-CQDS working solution was made by dilution of 1000 μL of the synthesized N,S-CQDS with water to 50 mL in a volumetric flask.

The synthesis procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1B. Microwave aided synthesis was used to create N,S co-doped CQDS as follows: After thoroughly triturating TSC (0.274 g, 3 mmol) and CA (0.192 g, 1 mmol) until obtaining a fine crystalline powder mixture, 5 mL distilled water was added. The mixture was placed in a home microwave for 3 minutes at 800 watts until a dark orange matrix was obtained. The product was allowed to cool to room temperature before being ultrasonically dissolved in 20 mL distilled water for 15 minutes. The dark orange solution was centrifuged for 20 minutes at 5000 rpm to remove insoluble debris. A 0.22 μm syringe filter was used to filter the resulting supernatant. The prepared N,S-CQDS solution was kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C in an amber-colored glass container for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared N,S -CQDS
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (Ф) of the generated N,S-CQDS was evaluated using quinine sulphate as a reference (prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution, 54% fluorescence quantum yield). To reduce the inner filter effect (IFE), standard solutions of quinine sulphate and N,S-CQDS were prepared to possess absorbance ˂ 0.05 at λex. The quantum yield (Ф) was calculated using the following Eq. (1) (Qandeel et al., 2023):
(1)
Ф x
=
Ф
s t
×
F x
F
s t
×
A
s t
A x
×
n x 2
n
s t
2
Where Ф is the quantum yield, F is the integrated fluorescence, A is the absorbance, and n is the solvent's refractive index. St and x symbols refer to standard quinine sulphate and N,S-CQDS, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for determining SNZ and TNZ
General procedures for constructing calibration curves
ce:section.ce:para:
To quantify fluorescence (F), 1500 μL of N,S-CQDS working solution was poured into two groups of 10 mL volumetric flasks, and increasing concentrations of each drug standard solution were added. The solutions were mixed thoroughly after completion of the final volumes with distilled water. The fluorescence intensity (FI) was measured at 360 nm and 430 nm excitation/emission wavelengths, respectively. The drugs were eliminated for the blank experiment measurement, and F
O (blank reading) was recorded. Each experiment was carried out three times. F
O
/F was plotted against [drug concentration] (μM) to plot the calibration curves. The calibration curves were used to generate regression equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SNZ and TNZ in their tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets were accurately weighed and triturated. The weight required to obtain a final concentration of 2 mM of each drug was transferred into 100 mL volumetric flasks. Fifty mL distilled water was added to both flasks. For complete extraction, the contents of both flasks were sonicated for 20 min, diluted to final volume with distilled water and filtered to remove insoluble excipients. The resultant clear solutions were transferred at various concentrations into a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks containing 1500 μL of N,S-CQDS solution then the procedure described in section 2.6.1 was applied for determination of the concentration of both drugs. The % recoveries and standard deviation were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SNZ and TNZ in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: The method of Sun et al. (2012) has been applied for protein precipitation with some modification. One mL of urine sample was spiked with different concentrations of the cited drugs, SNZ and TNZ. Four mL of acetonitrile was added to precipitate proteins. After vortex mixing for 10 minutes, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 minutes and the supernatant was carefully collected and filtered with 0.22 μm membrane filter. Qne mL of each supernatant was transferred in to 10 mL volumetric flask, mixed with 1500 μL of N,S-CQDS, and completed to 10 mL with water. A blank experiment was done omitting the step of drug spiking. The prepared solutions were further treated according to procedure described in section “2.6.1.“.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical features of N,S-CQDS synthesized with microwave assistance
ce:section.ce:para: For structural characterization, TEM imaging was used for exploring the size and the morphological nature of N,S-CQDS. Fig. 3 A shows spherical shaped particles with average size of 13.2 ± 4.3 nm. FTIR analysis was carried out to explore the chemistry and functional groups of the produced N,S-CQDS. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, N,S-CQDS exhibit at 3285 cm-1 band of N-H stretching vibration, and O-H stretching vibration band at 3182 cm-1. The peak at 2973 cm-1 is specified to C-H stretching vibration, and the peak at 2062 cm-1 refers to N-H stretching vibration. The strong narrow peaks at 1698 and 1622 cm-1 refer to C=O and C=C stretching vibrations, respectively. The two small peaks at 1072 and 1039 cm-1 are assigned to S=O stretching while the peak at 1227 cm-1 relates to C=S stretching. In addition to the previous peaks, O-H bending, or S-N stretching is the explanation for the peak at 925 cm-1. S-S bond stretching vibration appears at 568 cm-1 (Dutta, 2017). FTIR results confirm the successful synthesis of carbon dots with various oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surfaces which increase polarity and aqueous solubility.

EDX was employed in order to confirm the types and percent of atoms present in the produced N,S -CQDS. Fig. 3C reveals that the generated N,S-CQDS are composed of carbon (40.78%), nitrogen (42.25%), oxygen (13.93%), and sulfur (3.04%) indicating successful and high level of doping. Furthermore, Zeta potential (ζ) of the prepared N,S-CQDS was determined (Fig. 3D). Zeta potential (ζ) equals -32.46 ± 1.55 mv which means high repulsion among particles due to high surface negative charge, consequently high stability of the nano particles was achieved since repulsion prevent their aggregation keeping them in the nano size (Shah et al., 2014).

XPS measurements were used to analyze the surface composition of N,S-CQDs. Fig. 4 A shows XPS survey scan spectrum of the N,S-CQDS. The predominant peaks are identified as carbon (286.46 eV), oxygen (532.69 eV), nitrogen (400.82 eV), and sulfur (165.13 eV), which indicates successful doping of carbon quantum dots with sulfur and nitrogen. As shown in Fig. 4B, the high-resolution spectrum of N1s was deconvoluted into two peaks at 400.65 and 399.38 eV that are assigned to imide (C=O)-N-(C=O) and amine, C-NR2 (RC, H), respectively. The two peaks at 531.31 and 532.51 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of O1s refers to C-O-C/C-OH and C=O, respectively (Fig. 4C). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s represents three peaks at 284.55, 286.42, and 288.2 eV (Fig. 4D), that relate to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-S/C-N, and O-C=O/N-C=O, respectively. As seen in Fig. 4E, the high resolution spectrum of S2p exhibits four characteristic peaks at 161.72, 163.76, 164.99, and 167.95 eV indicate to S2−, SO₃2⁻, R-S(=O)(=O)-R′ and SO₂, respectively (Chastain et al., 1992). The XPS analysis revealed the doping of nitrogen and sulfur into CQDs and indicated that the N,S-CQDs contain abundant -COOH, -OH, -NR2, - S2−, and -SO₃2⁻ function groups.

UV-Visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were also used to demonstrate the optical characteristics of N,S-CQDS. The absorption spectrum of the synthesized carbon dots (Fig. 5 A) exhibits two characteristic peaks at 240 and 308 nm related to π- π* and n- π* transitions, respectively (Mikheev et al., 2018). Following excitation at 360 nm, the produced N,S-CQDS exhibits intense fluorescence at 430 nm. The fluorescence of quantum dots is attributed to conjugated π-domains and surface defects (Molaei, 2020). The defect sites are those that don't have a perfect sp2 domain. Surface energy traps are created by non-perfect locations. CQDs emit fluorescence due to sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and functionalized surface defects. Furthermore, functionalization and surface passivation of CQDs result in increased surface defect stabilization which will lead to easy radiative recombination of the electron/hole pairs, confined on the surface (Molaei, 2020).

The synthesized N,S-CQDS show excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior (Fig. 5B). The maximum emission wavelength did not change much when the excitation wavelength was changed from 300 to 340 nm. However, the FI gradually increases up to 340 nm. As the excitation wavelength is increased from 350 to 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelength red shifts and the FI gradually decreases. This excitation-dependent behavior initiates from surface functional groups communicating via the deformed sp2 carbon framework (Sharma et al., 2016). Using quinine sulphate as a reference, the quantum yield of the N,S-CQDS was determined to be 6.7%, suggesting good fluorescence quality.

When employing fluorescent materials as fluorescent sensors, their stability is crucial. In this study, the influence of pH on the fluorescence of N,S-CQDS was examined using Britton Robinson buffer (BRB) over the pH range of 2.0-12.0. Fig. 5C demonstrates that the FI of N,S-CQDS decreases somewhat at pH levels below 3.0 and above 9.0. The lower fluorescence at pH below 3.0 is attributed to protonation of the amino group on the surface of N,S-CQDS which enhances the ability of intramolecular electron transfer between protonated amino group and quantum dots, resulting in decreased photoluminescence. On the other hand, at pH over 9.0, the excited electrons of N,S-CQDS can move from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Due to the fact that HOMO energy level of NH2 is greater than that of CQDS, the ground state electrons of amino group may hop to HOMO of C-dots, causing fluorescence reduction (Liu et al., 2021).

The stability of CQDS under UV light was also explored and it was found that the FI decreased by only about 5% after 30 min of exposure to UV light at 365 nm. Furthermore, the N,S-CQDS were stable for more than 4 months of storage at 4 °C showing constant fluorescence without any apparent turbidity or precipitation. These findings demonstrate the N,S-CQDS' outstanding stability, allowing for interesting use as fluorescent probes for drugs determination over a wide variety of pharmaceuticals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SNZ and TNZ determination using N,S-CQDS as a fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDS at 430 nm steadily decreased upon addition of concentrations ranging from (15-200 μM) of both drugs SNZ and TNZ (Fig. 6 A&B, respectively). Different steps have been performed to determine the quenching mechanism (El-Shaheny et al., 2020). First, the quenching mechanism was illustrated through exploring the possible overlapping between excitation, emission spectra of N,S-CQDS and the absorption spectra of SNZ and TNZ. As shown in Fig. 5A, the absorption spectra of the investigated drugs are overlapping only with the excitation spectrum of the sensor, consequently possible primary inner filter effect contributes to fluorescence quenching (Chen et al., 2018). For further investigation of the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDS in presence of various concentrations of SNZ and TNZ was corrected by subtracting the IFE (F
corrected
) using the following Eq. (2) (Chen et al., 2018):
(2)
F
c o r r e c t e d
=
F
o b s e r v e d
× a n t i l o g
(
A
e x
+
A
e m
2
)
Where F
corrected is the fluorescence intensity after subtracting the IFE, F
observed is the observed FI, A
ex and A
em are the absorbance of quenchers at excitation and emission wavelengths of CQDS, respectively.

The % suppressed efficiency (%E) of both corrected and observed FI has been calculated using Eq. (3) (Abdallah et al., 2022):
(3)
% E =
[
1 −
(
F
F 0
)
]
× 100

As shown in Fig. 7 A & B, by subtracting the IFE from the observed fluorescence intensity of N,S -CQDS in presence of quenchers, %E decreased greatly indicating that IFE was the main contributor in fluorescence quenching by about 75% and 80% in case of SNZ and TNZ,respectively. Yet, there is another mechanism responsible for the remaining percentage of quenching. As a result, we used Stern-Volmer analysis at three different temperatures (293, 303, 313K) to define this quenching mechanism using the following Eq. (4) (Gehlen, 2020):
(4)
F 0
/ F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
Where K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the quencher molar concentration. The corrected fluorescence intensity (F
corrected
) was used in all calculations. By plotting F
0
/F against molar concentration of both drugs at three different temperatures (293, 303, 313 K) (Fig. 7C & D), it was found that the slope value (K
SV
) decreased as the temperature increased (Table 2 ) indicating that the remaining percent of quenching is a static quenching mechanism (Gehlen, 2020). For further confirmation of the static quenching mechanism, the absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDS was measured in absence and presence of SNZ and TNZ. As seen in Fig. 7E, the absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDS changed in the presence of SNZ and TNZ, that in turn confirms the formation of a new complex between the N,S-CQDS and the investigated drugs. Finally, we can conclude that SNZ and TNZ quench the FI of N,S-CQDS through inner filter effect and static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure optimization for SNZ and TNZ determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The highest sensitivity for determination of SNZ and TNZ was achieved through investigating the effect of different experimental factors on the analytical method (Fig. 8 ). Firstly, the effect of pH was explored using BRB over the pH range of 2.0-12.0. It was found that the highest values for F
0
/F ratio was obtained over the pH range of 7.0 to 10.0 for both drugs. While the F
0
/F ratio slightly decreased at more acidic pH, 2.0-4.0 (Fig. 8A). These findings are strongly correlated to the UV-Visible absorption spectra of SNZ and TNZ at different pH values. As seen in Fig. 8C & 8D, the absorption spectra of both drugs exhibit higher absorbance value in neutral and alkaline pHs that significantly decreased in acidic pH. This behavior is probably attributed to the tautomeric form of the drugs that are formed under basic condition (Fig. 8 E). The extended conjugation in the tautomeric form, known as nitronates (Breuer et al., 2008), enhances the absorbance of the two drugs, thus boosting the quenching of N,S-CQDS fluorescence by IFE. So, determination of the investigated drugs was completed at pH 7.0 without using a buffer for simplicity and analyst safety.

Secondly, the effect of N,S-CQDS amount on the method sensitivity was investigated using volumes of 300-1700 μL. The optimal amount of N,S-CQDS for both medications was 1500 μL since it had the highest F
0
/F ratio (Fig. 8B).

N,S-CQDS were evaluated for their selectivity for determination of SNZ and TNZ. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDS was recorded in the presence of several co-existing substances including Ca2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, sodium citrate, citric acid, carboxy methyl cellulose, glucose, and sucrose. In addition, some co-administered drugs from various pharmacological classifications, including spiramycin, paracetamol, and aceclofenac, were tested to see how they affect the FI of the constructed sensor. As demonstrated in Table 6, the existence of these substances has no substantial effect on the fluorescent probe's FI, with the percent change in FI ranging from -5.08 to +5.479%, showing the method's selectivity and reliability for pharmaceutical applications.

The proposed method was evaluated as stated by ICH guidelines (Walfish, 2006). The intensity of N,S-CQDS fluorescence was recorded in the existence of various concentrations of SNZ and TNZ. F
0
/F was plotted against concentration of drugs and excellent linearity was achieved over concentrations ranging from 15 to 200 μM of SNZ and TNZ that correlated to the following illustrated regression equations:
(5)
F
0
/F = 0.0048[SNZ] + 0.9615 (r = 0.9999)
(6)
F
0
/F = 0.0034[TNZ] + 1.0058 (r = 0.9999)
where [SNZ], [TNZ] are secnidazole and tinidazole concentration [μM], respectively and r is the correlation coefficient. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were determined as 3.3σ/S, and 10σ/S, respectively, where σ is the standard deviation of the response and S is the slope of the calibration curve. LOD and LOQ for determination of SNZ equal 2.18 and 6.62 μM, respectively, LOD and LOQ in case of determination of TNZ equal 2.3 and 6.96 μM, respectively (Table 3 ).

The accuracy of the method was explored through statistically comparing the proposed assay findings with those gathered using comparison methods (El Walily et al., 2000; Lopez-Martinez et al., 1997) using student t-test and variance ratio F-test. It was concluded that there is no significant difference between the proposed and reported methods because the calculated t-test and F-test values are less than the tabulated values (Miller and Miller, 2018) (Table 4 ).

Besides, the precision of the method was evaluated through assaying of three concentrations of SNZ and TNZ three times in the same day (intra-day precision) and on three successive days (inter-day precision) (Walfish, 2006). The method shows high precision as the calculated % RSD are less than 2% (Miller and Miller, 2018) (Table 5
). The validation results suggest the validity of the designed method and its applicability for real pharmaceutical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SNZ and TNZ in their commercial tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The method was evaluated for its ability and efficacy for determination of SNZ and TNZ in their commercially available tablets. The high % found (99.1-101.6 and 98.22-99.16 for SNZ and TNZ, respectively) and small SD (±1.35 and ±0.47 for SNZ and TNZ, respectively (Table 4) confirm the suggested method's ability and applicability for determining SNZ and TNZ without interfering from typical additives and excipients. The results of analysis of the two cited drugs in their commercial dosage forms were compared with the results obtained by comparison methods (El Walily et al., 2000; Lopez-Martinez et al., 1997) by student t-test and variance ratio F-test (Miller and Miller, 2018) showing no significant difference between the methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SNZ and TNZ in spiked human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: SNZ and TNZ are orally administered and excreted mainly as unchanged drugs in the urine (Wisher, 2012). Based on this information and the LODs of the proposed method, it was suggested that the effective detection of both drugs in human urine could be achieved (Fig. 9 A & B). The collected urine was used as a blank before spiking with drugs. The recovery of the two drugs was determined by adding a known concentration of each drug to separate urine samples. The samples were processed as described in section “2.6.3.” and the absolute %recovery was calculated. As shown in Table 7 , the average absolute % recovery was 102.24 ± 2.93 and 98.2 ± 1.78 for SNZ and TNZ, respectively. The high %recovery indicates that the proposed approach can be effectively utilized to determine the analyzed drugs in human urine with excellent selectivity without significant interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the N,S-CQDS fluorescence sensor approach with the methods published for the investigated drugs
ce:section.ce:para: Comparison of the proposed method for determination of SNZ and TNZ with the published methods showed many points of strength and many advantages that allow favorable application in pharmaceutical field (Table 8 ). The reported HPLC methods for determination of SNZ and TNZ (El Walily et al., 2000; Li et al., 2007; Walash et al., 2013; Yin et al., 2013) using hazardous and expensive chemicals like methanol and acetonitrile. Furthermore, they require time for equilibration and mobile phase preparation beside the long analysis time while the proposed method is simple rapid and avoids using such hazardous chemicals. SNZ was determined spectrophotometrically (Saffaj et al., 2006) however the analytical procedures are not ecofriendly due to inclusion of preparative work for sampling such as reduction and then coupling with toxic phenolic reagent (β-naphthol). The published spectrophotometric method for determination of TNZ (Adegoke et al., 2010) are less sensitive than the reported method and including the use of several toxic solvents and chemicals like methanol, ethyl acetate, ethanol, sulfuric acid, chloroform, and acetone while the reported spectrophotometric method (Lopez-Martinez et al., 1997) uses severe drastic conditions including boiling water bath for sample preparation. The reported electrochemical method for determination of TNZ (Sawkar et al., 2022) requires preparative work for electrode preparation and consumes harsh solvents and chemicals. The suggested method is more advantageous than the published fluorometric methods (Yang et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022; Magdy et al., 2023) as the reported methods synthesize CDS via hydrothermal method which require high temperature more than 180°C and longer time exceeding 24 h. Hence from the presented comparison, the proposed method shows safe, rapid, simple, sensitive, and green application for determination of SNZ and TNZ in pharmaceutical preparations.

The developed method's greenness, safety, and economic impact are clearly apparent due to the use of low energy and time-saving synthesis techniques, as well as safe carbon, sulfur, and nitrogen sources. In this regard, the most contemporary tool and software for evaluating the greenness of analytical procedures, known as the complementary green analytical procedure index (Complex GAPI) (Płotka-Wasylka et al., 2021), which is primarily aimed at assessing the eco-friendliness of analytical techniques, including pre-analysis processes and materials used before the analytical phase, has been used to evaluate the suggested approach. The outcome is depicted in the pictogram seen in Fig. 10 A. The hexagon at the bottom represents the green aspects of pre-analysis operations such as condition evaluation, reagents, solvents, instruments, occupational hazard, work up, and final product purification. The top pentagonal fields are related to the green characteristics of the analytical approach itself, such as sample preparation procedures, amounts of reagents and solvents used while considering their health and safety hazards, instrumentation, energy consumption, waste generated and the method of waste treatment, and method nature (qualitative/quantitative). The colors green, yellow, and red represent low, medium, and high environmental dangers associated with each step of the pre-analysis practices and the analytical process, respectively. Fig. 10A depicts the environmental friendliness of both the N,S-CQDS synthesis process and the analytical approach. The Analytical greenness calculator (AGREE) (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020) was also utilized to evaluate the greenness of the created approach by investigating sample processes, the amount and toxicity of used chemicals and solvents, energy required, and waste produced. The overall analysis score is 0.63 (Fig. 10B) suggesting that the approach is green.

When compared to the published hydrothermal methods, the findings of the two-greenness metrics demonstrate that the suggested approach exhibits outstanding greenness characteristics since the synthesis of N,S-CQDS fluorescent probes is a one-pot process completed in a very short time with minimal energy consumption. The suggested technique is both cost-effective and environmentally benign because it relies on a readily available microwave oven, avoids the use of harmful organic solvents, and the analysis procedures are straightforward. The previously mentioned criteria improve the suggested method's greenness and facilitate its application for SNZ and TNZ determination.

This work succeeded in presenting rapid, economic, sensitive, and effective method for preparing fluorescent N,S-CQDS starting with TSC and CA via one-step and one-pot route within 3 min using home available domestic microwave. The structure of the prepared nano sensors has been characterized using TEM imaging, IR spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, energy dispersive-X-ray spectroscopy analysis (EDX) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties were evaluated using UV-Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. N,S-CQDS showed excellent aqueous solubility with high stability for more than 4 months of storage at 4 °C and fluorescence quantum yield of 6.7%. The produced N,S-CQDS were effectively applied as a fluorescent sensor for quantification of SNZ and TNZ in their commercially available tablets with %recoveries ≥ 99.66% with SD not exceeding ± 1.35. Furthermore, the designed sensor was applied for determination of SNZ and TNZ in human urine samples with average % recoveries of 102.24±2.93% and 98.2±1.78%, respectively. The proposed validated method excludes the use of hazardous solvents and consumes little energy which makes it an excellent choice for pharmaceutical applications and routine quality control practices.
Mohamed M. Osman: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - Original Draft.
Rania El-Shaheny: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Visualization, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing.
Fawzia A. Ibrahim: Conceptualization, Resources, Validation, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Sharma et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origin of excitation dependent fluorescence in carbon nanodots
J. Phys. Chem. Lett.
